plainMeaning: A devout great-souled king named Puṇyarāja, out of love for Lord Mahāvīra, had an image of him made in the thirty-seventh year after Mahāvīra was born.
recitation: to punnarāya nāmā, koi mahappā jiṇassa bhattie | kārai paḍimaṁ varise, sagatīse vīra jammāo ||

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. tataḥ / tadā ('then, from that'); a narrative connective.
wordByWord.notes: Picks up the scene from the preceding verse (Vīra standing in pratimā at Muṇḍasthala) and moves to its founding-record.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. Puṇya-rāja ('merit-king') or Pūrṇa-rāja ('full king') → Pkt. punna-rāya.
wordByWord.notes: The traditional records of this tīrtha render the name as Pūrṇarāja / Puṇyarāja (King Purnaraj/Punyaraj).

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. nāman ('name'), here in the adverbial 'by name' sense.
Skt. ko'pi / kaścit ('some, a certain') → Pkt. koi.
wordByWord.notes: Indefinite; introduces the patron without further identification, as legend rather than dated chronicle.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. mahātman ('great-souled') → Pkt. mahappā (tm > pp assimilation).
wordByWord.notes: A title of pious esteem for the donor-king.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. jinasya (genitive of jina, 'conqueror') → Pkt. jiṇassa.
wordByWord.notes: Genitive governing 'devotion'; the Jina here is Mahāvīra, named explicitly later as Vīra.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. bhaktyā (instrumental of bhakti, 'devotion') → Pkt. bhattie.
wordByWord.notes: Instrumental of cause: devotion, not display, is named as the king's motive.

wordByWord.gloss: has (an image) made, causes to be made
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. kārayati (causative of √kṛ, 'to do/make') → Pkt. kārai.
wordByWord.notes: Causative: the king commissions, he does not carve. Historic present used for the past act of image-donation.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. pratimā ('image, likeness') → Pkt. paḍimā, accusative paḍimaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: The object made; at Muṇḍasthala this is the standing kāyotsarga image of Mahāvīra.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. varṣe (locative of varṣa, 'year/rain') → Pkt. varise.
wordByWord.notes: Locative of time, governing the numeral that follows.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. saptatriṁśat (37) → Pkt. sattatīsa / sagatīsa; locative sagatīse.
wordByWord.notes: The verse's pivotal figure: 37 years counted from Vīra's birth.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. Vīra ('hero'), short for Mahāvīra; in compound with the following ablative.
Skt. janmataḥ / janmanaḥ (ablative of janman, 'birth') → Pkt. jammāo (nm > mm).
wordByWord.notes: Ablative fixing the reckoning-point: counted onward from the birth of Mahāvīra.

literalTranslation: Then a certain great soul, Puṇyarāja by name, through devotion to the Jina has an image made — in the year thirty-seven from Vīra's birth.
idiomaticTranslation: Then a certain great-souled king, Puṇyarāja by name, moved by devotion to the Jina, had this image fashioned in the thirty-seventh year reckoned from the birth of Vīra.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where v.97 left Mahāvīra standing motionless in pratimā (kāyotsarga) at Muṇḍasthala while still chadmastha, this verse supplies the shrine's founding-record: who made its image, and when. The 'great soul named Puṇyarāja' is the devotee-king, and the causative kārai marks him as patron — one who has the image commissioned, not one who carves it, the standard idiom of Jain image-donation. The motive is named with care: bhattie, 'out of devotion' to the Jina, not for fame. The verse's most arresting claim is its date — varise sagatīse vīra jammāo, 'in the thirty-seventh year from Vīra's birth.' Since tradition has Mahāvīra renounce at thirty and reach kevala-jñāna near forty-two, year thirty-seven falls squarely within his chadmastha wandering. The image was therefore consecrated while the Jina yet lived and was not yet omniscient — which is precisely why the previous verse portrayed him as chadmastha. The records of this tīrtha add that Keśī-svāmī performed the consecration. The poet's purpose is evidentiary: by fixing an exact year he authenticates Muṇḍasthala's extraordinary boast — an image of Mahāvīra made from life, among the oldest anywhere — and so leads directly into v.99's reckoning of the tīrtha's age ('somewhat less than eighteen hundred years').
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse quietly legitimizes mūrti-pūjā (image-veneration), central to the Mūrtipūjaka tradition, by rooting the practice in the Jina's own lifetime rather than a later age. The chadmastha framing rests on the doctrine of the four ghātī-karmas: until they are destroyed at kevala-jñāna, even a Tīrthaṅkara remains chadmastha — 'veiled' — so an image made in year thirty-seven honours the still-striving ascetic, not yet the omniscient teacher. Devotion (bhakti) is presented as the efficient cause of a merit-bearing (puṇya) act: commissioning an image is itself a meritorious deed when its root is reverence.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Naming Puṇyarāja's devotion-driven act, the reciter is invited to see image-veneration as an unbroken chain reaching back to Mahāvīra's own days, and to take the king as a model — letting bhakti, not display or status, be the true motive behind any act of worship or giving.

crossReferences.text: Aṣṭottarī Tīrthamālā vv.97 and 99 (the framing of this Muṇḍasthala triad)

sources.title: Mudsthal Tirth (Mungthala) — history and the Punyaraj / Keshiswami legend
sources.consultedFor: King Pūrṇarāja/Puṇyarāja commissioning Mahāvīra's image in his 37th year, consecration by Keśī-svāmī, and the Aṣṭottarī Tīrthamālā attribution

sources.title: Mungathala
sources.consultedFor: Identification of Muṇḍasthala/Mungthala (Sirohi, Rajasthan), the standing kāyotsarga mūlanāyaka, and medieval inscriptional history

sources.title: Shri Mundasthal Tirth — Jain Temples in Rajasthan
sources.consultedFor: Temple description and the tradition of an image consecrated in Mahāvīra's lifetime

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit roots underlying the glosses: pratimā, varṣa, janman, kārayati (caus. √kṛ), bhakti, saptatriṁśat

notes: The text and numeral are clear: sagatīse = 37 (Pkt. sattatīsa/sagatīsa, Skt. saptatriṁśat). 'Punnarāya' is rendered Pūrṇarāja/Puṇyarāja in the tīrtha's traditional records (King Purnaraj/Punyaraj); both Sanskrit readings (pūrṇa- 'full' / puṇya- 'merit') are defensible — flagged as mild uncertainty. The verb kārai is a causative historic-present for a past act of image-donation. The date 'year 37 from Vīra's birth' places the consecration within Mahāvīra's chadmastha period, harmonizing with v.97's portrayal of him as chadmastha; the detail that Keśī-svāmī performed the consecration comes from the tīrtha tradition, not from the verse itself. Book orthography preserved verbatim per fidelity rules.
